page_content:
[Context: Virtual Environments > Why Deactivate a Virtual Environment]

## Why Deactivate a Virtual Environment
For example, you could be working on a project [CODE: philosophers-stone], [BOLD:activate that virtual environment], install packages and work with that environment.
And then you want to work on [BOLD:another project] [CODE: prisoner-of-azkaban].
You go to that project:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ cd ~/code/prisoner-of-azkaban...]

If you don't deactivate the virtual environment for [CODE: philosophers-stone], when you run [CODE: python] in the terminal, it will try to use the Python from [CODE: philosophers-stone].

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: $ cd ~/code/prisoner-of-azkaban $ python main.py /...]

But if you deactivate the virtual environment and activate the new one for [CODE: prisoner-of-askaban] then when you run [CODE: python] it will use the Python from the virtual environment in [CODE: prisoner-of-azkaban].

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: $ cd ~/code/prisoner-of-azkaban // You don't need ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Virtual Environments > Why Deactivate a Virtual Environment

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Callbacks > Write the callback documentation code]

## Write the callback documentation code
This code won't be executed in your app, we only need it to [ITALIC:document] how that [ITALIC:external API] should look like.
But, you already know how to easily create automatic documentation for an API with [BOLD:FastAPI].
So we are going to use that same knowledge to document how the [ITALIC:external API] should look like... by creating the [ITALIC:path operation(s)] that the external API should implement (the ones your API will call).
/// tip
When writing the code to document a callback, it might be useful to imagine that you are that [ITALIC:external developer]. And that you are currently implementing the [ITALIC:external API], not [ITALIC:your API].
Temporarily adopting this point of view (of the [ITALIC:external developer]) can help you feel like it's more obvious where to put the parameters, the Pydantic model for the body, for the response, etc. for that [ITALIC:external API].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: OpenAPI Callbacks > Write the callback documentation code

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Query parameter list / multiple values > Query parameter list / multiple values with defaults]

### Query parameter list / multiple values with defaults
You can also define a default [CODE: list] of values if none are provided:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:items(q: Annotated[list[str], Query()] = ["foo", "bar"]):
query]items = {"q": q}
```
If you go to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: http://localhost:8000/items/...]
the default of [CODE: q] will be: [CODE: ["foo", "bar"]] and your response will be:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "q": [ "foo", "bar" ] }...]
#### Using just [CODE: list]
You can also use [CODE: list] directly instead of [CODE: list[str]]:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:items(q: Annotated[list, Query()] = []):
query]items = {"q": q}
```
/// note
Keep in mind that in this case, FastAPI won't check the contents of the list.
For example, [CODE: list[int]] would check (and document) that the contents of the list are integers. But [CODE: list] alone wouldn't.
///

hierarchy: Query parameter list / multiple values with defaults
metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Query parameter list / multiple values > Query parameter list / multiple values with defaults

page_content:
[Context: Get Current User > Other models]

## Other models
You can now get the current user directly in the [ITALIC:path operation functions] and deal with the security mechanisms at the [BOLD:Dependency Injection] level, using [CODE: Depends].
And you can use any model or data for the security requirements (in this case, a Pydantic model [CODE: User]).
But you are not restricted to using some specific data model, class or type.
Do you want to have an [CODE: id] and [CODE: email] and not have any [CODE: username] in your model? Sure. You can use these same tools.
Do you want to just have a [CODE: str]? Or just a [CODE: dict]? Or a database class model instance directly? It all works the same way.
You actually don't have users that log in to your application but robots, bots, or other systems, that have just an access token? Again, it all works the same.
Just use any kind of model, any kind of class, any kind of database that you need for your application. [BOLD:FastAPI] has you covered with the dependency injection system.

[Context: OAuth2 scopes > OAuth2 scopes and OpenAPI]

## OAuth2 scopes and OpenAPI
The OAuth2 specification defines "scopes" as a list of strings separated by spaces.
The content of each of these strings can have any format, but should not contain spaces.
These scopes represent "permissions".
In OpenAPI (e.g. the API docs), you can define "security schemes".
When one of these security schemes uses OAuth2, you can also declare and use scopes.
Each "scope" is just a string (without spaces).
They are normally used to declare specific security permissions, for example:
[ITALIC: [CODE: users:read] or [CODE: users:write] are common examples.
] [CODE: instagram_basic] is used by Facebook / Instagram.
- [CODE: https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive] is used by Google.
/// info
In OAuth2 a "scope" is just a string that declares a specific permission required.
It doesn't matter if it has other characters like [CODE: :] or if it is a URL.
Those details are implementation specific.
For OAuth2 they are just strings.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: OAuth2 scopes > OAuth2 scopes and OpenAPI

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > Verify the `username` and data shape]

## Verify the [CODE: username] and data shape
We verify that we get a [CODE: username], and extract the scopes.
And then we validate that data with the Pydantic model (catching the [CODE: ValidationError] exception), and if we get an error reading the JWT token or validating the data with Pydantic, we raise the [CODE: HTTPException] we created before.
For that, we update the Pydantic model [CODE: TokenData] with a new property [CODE: scopes].
By validating the data with Pydantic we can make sure that we have, for example, exactly a [CODE: list] of [CODE: str] with the scopes and a [CODE: str] with the [CODE: username].
Instead of, for example, a [CODE: dict], or something else, as it could break the application at some point later, making it a security risk.
We also verify that we have a user with that username, and if not, we raise that same exception we created before.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: scopes: list[str] = [] detail="Could not validate ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: OAuth2 scopes > Verify the `username` and data shape
